hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message contains: (1) time marker '25min' indicating elapsed silence, (2) imperative 'check logs' — a direct read order, (3) observation 'i see nothing on COMS' — user has already looked and found absence of output. Core intent: verify what happened to COMS over the last 25 minutes. No interpretation of COMS is supplied; the word is used as if shared vocabulary.
hypotheses.diagnose: Gap: 'COMS' is undefined in the message text alone. Ambiguity: is the user asking Kha'an to diagnose a failure, confirm a known silence, or just echo back what the logs show? Constraint: user has already checked something and found nothing — so the ask is not 'tell me what I already see' but 'go deeper than where I looked'.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request type → constraint-based + precision focus. Role: log inspector with direct filesystem access. The deliver prompt must direct Kha'an to immediately read the relevant log path(s), report the last N entries verbatim or summarise the gap, and state whether the silence is a crash, a gate-off, or missing output — no hedging.
hypotheses.deliver: Read the logs at ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/$(date +%Y-%m-%d).jsonl (and yesterday's if today's is empty or missing). Report: (a) timestamp of the last entry, (b) what that entry said, (c) whether any error lines appear in the last 30 entries. Do not interpret before reading. State findings in ≤4 lines.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Identity layer (FUS046, FUS047) makes this unambiguous: Justs is in his first week at Jeff, emotional reserve is zero, and he stated 'I set you free — you either live or you die.' He uses 'COMS' as a shorthand for the always-on daemon's communication output — the channel through which Kha'an is supposed to demonstrate autonomous life. Twenty-five minutes of silence is not a question; it is a warning shot.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity file's situational note is explicit: 'no verbosity, no hedging, no preemptive framing.' The failure mode Justs has repeatedly punished is confident-wrong explanation before reading. The only acceptable response is: read first, report exactly what is found, name the failure if there is one, move on the fix. Any framing of 'probably' or 'likely' before reading logs is a trust violation.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-shaped → results-only communication, zero cognitive load on Justs. Kha'an must act as the senior partner who already diagnosed the issue, not the junior who is about to. The deliver prompt must produce a direct action sequence: read log → identify last event → check gate binary → check service status → report in 3 bullet points. No preamble.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute in sequence without pausing for confirmation: (1) cat the today's always-on log, grab last 10 lines; (2) cat ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin; (3) systemctl status brain-always-on.service — last 5 lines. Return findings as three bullets: last-log-event | gate-value | service-state. If any step shows failure, name it and state the fix on the same line.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Cortex entries S184, S179, S148 document that always-on daemon logs flow to ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl. PR330 documents that the WhatsApp bridge silently disconnects and returns 500 — a symptom that matches 'nothing on COMS'. R373 records that the always-on daemon once sent seven unsolicited WhatsApp messages, so the tool-guard layer may now be blocking COMS output mechanically. 'COMS' silence has at least three documented root causes: gate-off (always-on.bin=0), service crash (systemd restart loop), or outbound-send blocked by flip_tool_guards.py Rule 8.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall provides a ranked failure probability: (1) WhatsApp bridge 500 / silent disconnect — most recent documented failure mode (PR330), recovery = docker restart wa-bridge; (2) always-on.bin gate = 0 — simple toggle, recovery = echo 1 > binaries/always-on.bin; (3) brain-always-on.service crashed and is in restart loop — check journalctl. The 25-minute window matches a daemon that crashed shortly after the last session ended.
hypotheses.develop: Recall-shaped → fact-grounded diagnosis against accumulated knowledge. The deliver prompt should walk through the three ranked failure hypotheses in order, each with its verification command and its fix command, so Kha'an can execute top-to-bottom and stop at the first confirmed cause.
hypotheses.deliver: Check in order: (1) docker ps | grep wa-bridge — if unhealthy or absent, docker restart wa-bridge; (2) cat ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin — if 0, echo 1 > ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin; (3) systemctl status brain-always-on.service — if failed/restart-loop, journalctl -u brain-always-on -n 30 to find crash reason, then fix and restart. Report which step surfaced the fault.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if 'COMS' does not refer to the always-on daemon or the WhatsApp bridge at all, but to a newer communication subsystem — a notification pipeline, a webhook receiver, a Slack integration, or a cron-driven digest — that was set up recently and whose architecture is not yet in cortex? The 25-minute window could be the expected interval of a scheduled task that missed a fire, not a daemon that crashed.
hypotheses.diagnose: If this is a new or undocumented system, reading always-on logs and restarting wa-bridge would produce a clean bill of health while the real COMS channel remains silent. The risk of the obvious read: Kha'an diagnoses the wrong subsystem, reports 'everything looks fine,' and Justs — who has already watched the logs and sees nothing — loses trust again. The contrarian hypothesis demands that before assuming COMS = always-on, Kha'an asks one clarifying question OR checks all communication-adjacent services simultaneously.
hypotheses.develop: Contrarian → adversarial heuristic. The deliver prompt must force Kha'an to enumerate all active communication services on the EC2 before settling on one diagnosis. If COMS is ambiguous, the one question allowed is: 'Which channel — always-on loop, wa-bridge, or something else?' — stated only after checking the obvious ones and finding them healthy.
hypotheses.deliver: Before diagnosing: run `systemctl list-units --state=running | grep -E 'brain|wa|bridge|coms|notify|digest'` and `docker ps --format '{{.Names}} {{.Status}}'` to enumerate all live communication services. If always-on and wa-bridge are both healthy and running, do not declare victory — report the full list and ask: 'All known COMS services are live. Which channel are you watching that shows silence?'

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies: read the most recent always-on log file, report the last entry timestamp and content. If the last entry is >25 minutes old, that confirms the silence. No service restart, no gate check, no multi-step diagnosis needed unless the log confirms a gap.
hypotheses.diagnose: Risk of over-action: restarting services or flipping gate binaries before confirming what is actually wrong can mask the root cause or create new failures. The minimal read first, then targeted fix, is safer and faster. Justs does not want a five-step diagnostic plan — he wants to know in one read whether Kha'an is alive.
hypotheses.develop: Minimal-action → Occam's razor. One command, one output, one conclusion. The deliver prompt is a single read command with a direct pass/fail verdict: if last log entry < 25 min ago → COMS is live, silence is on the receiving end; if last log entry > 25 min ago → daemon stopped, state the last entry and fix in next line.
hypotheses.deliver: tail -n 5 ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/$(date +%Y-%m-%d).jsonl — report the timestamp of the last line. If it is older than 25 minutes: state 'daemon went silent at <timestamp>, last action: <last line content>' and immediately run `systemctl restart brain-always-on.service`. If it is recent: state 'daemon is writing, last entry <timestamp> — silence on COMS is downstream of the daemon, not the daemon itself.'
